questions.question: An array of 25 distinct elements is to be sorted using quicksort. Assume that the pivot element is chosen uniformly at random. The probability that the pivot element gets placed in the worst possible location in the first round of partitioning (rounded off to 2 decimal places) is ________
questions.to_find: Probability that pivot lands in worst possible position in the first partition

understand.plain: We have 25 distinct elements and pick a pivot uniformly at random. The worst case for quicksort happens when the pivot ends up at the very first or very last position of the sorted partition (i.e., it is either the minimum or the maximum element). There are 2 such worst positions out of 25 possible positions. So the probability is 2/25 = 0.08.

keywords.explain: A pivot is in the worst location when it produces the most unbalanced split — all elements on one side and none on the other. This happens when the pivot is the minimum (placed at index 0) or the maximum (placed at last index).
keywords.example: In [3,1,4,2,5], if pivot=5 it goes to the last position with 4 elements on one side and 0 on the other — worst split.

keywords.explain: Each of the n elements is equally likely to be chosen as pivot, so each has probability 1/n.
keywords.example: For n=4, each element has probability 1/4 = 0.25 of being selected.

understand.visual_alt: Array of 25 elements; only the 2 extreme positions (min and max) represent worst-case pivot placements out of 25 equally likely choices.

given.aim: Find the probability that a uniformly random pivot lands in a worst-case position.

terms.meaning: Total number of distinct elements in the array
terms.example: Array has 25 slots: index 0 to 24 after partitioning
terms.connects: Denominator of the probability: 25 possible pivot final positions

terms.meaning: Positions where pivot causes the most unbalanced split — either rank 1 (minimum) or rank 25 (maximum)
terms.example: If pivot is the min, all 24 others go to the right partition; 0 go to left.
terms.connects: There are exactly 2 such elements, giving numerator = 2

terms.meaning: Every element has equal probability 1/n of being chosen as pivot
terms.connects: Probability of choosing one of the 2 worst elements = 2/25

given.plan: Identify how many elements lead to the worst-case partition split (minimum and maximum element), then divide by total number of elements since pivot is chosen uniformly at random.

steps.title: Identify worst-case pivot positions
steps.formula_raw: Worst case occurs when pivot is rank 1 (minimum) or rank n (maximum)
steps.apply: For an array of 25 elements, the pivot produces the worst split if it is the smallest element (goes to position 0, leaving 24 on right, 0 on left) or the largest element (goes to position 24, leaving 0 on right, 24 on left). There are exactly 2 such elements.
steps.note: These are the only two positions that cause a completely unbalanced (0, n-1) split — the definition of worst case for quicksort.

steps.title: Compute the probability
steps.note: Rounded to 2 decimal places: 0.08. This is the required answer.

questions.formula_note: Worst-case quicksort partition occurs when pivot is the global minimum or maximum, giving probability 2/n for a uniform random pivot selection.

questions.question:
Consider sorting the following array of integers in ascending order using an inplace Quicksort algorithm that uses the last element as the pivot.

60  70  80  90  100

The minimum number of swaps performed during this Quicksort is ________
questions.to_find: Minimum number of swaps performed by in-place Quicksort (Lomuto, last-element pivot) on [60, 70, 80, 90, 100]

understand.plain: The array [60, 70, 80, 90, 100] is already sorted in ascending order. When Lomuto partition scheme is used with the last element as pivot, we scan the array and only swap when an element is out of place relative to the pivot. Since every element is already in its correct sorted position, no element needs to move — the number of swaps is 0.

keywords.explain: A quicksort variant that picks the last element as pivot and maintains a boundary index i. It swaps arr[i] with arr[j] when arr[j] <= pivot, then finally places the pivot at i+1.
keywords.example: For [2, 1, 3] with pivot=3: no elements trigger swaps; pivot swaps with itself (arr[2]).

keywords.explain: When the input is sorted in ascending order and we use the last element as pivot, the entire sub-array to the left is always smaller — no internal swaps happen, and the pivot swap is a self-swap (same index).
keywords.example: [1,2,3] with pivot=3: i ends at index 1, swap arr[2] with arr[2] — self-swap, counts as 0 actual swaps.

understand.visual_alt: Array [60, 70, 80, 90, 100] all elements already in ascending order; each recursive Lomuto call finds the pivot already in its correct position without moving any other element.

given.aim: Count the swaps performed by Lomuto in-place Quicksort on an already sorted array of 5 elements.

terms.meaning: Input array, already sorted in ascending order
terms.connects: Because it is sorted, every element is already less than the pivot at each recursive level

terms.meaning: Lomuto scheme: pivot = arr[hi]; index i starts at lo-1; for each j in [lo, hi-1], if arr[j] <= pivot then i++, swap arr[i] and arr[j]; finally swap arr[i+1] and arr[hi]
terms.example: For [60,70,80,90], pivot=90: all 3 elements are less, i ends at 2, swap arr[3] with arr[3] — no actual displacement
terms.connects: Self-swaps (same source and target index) do not move any data; minimum swap count = 0

terms.meaning: We count only swaps that exchange two distinct positions; a swap of arr[k] with arr[k] does not change the array
terms.example: swap(arr[4], arr[4]) in the first call — same index, 0 useful swaps
terms.connects: Across all 5 recursive calls (one per element), no element changes its index, giving total = 0

given.plan: Trace the Lomuto partition on [60,70,80,90,100]. At each recursive level the sub-array is already sorted, so every element satisfies arr[j] < pivot: i advances to hi-1, and the only swap is the pivot with itself (same index). Count 0 meaningful swaps per level and sum.

steps.title: Trace top-level partition on [60,70,80,90,100]
steps.formula_raw: Lomuto: i = lo-1; for j in [lo..hi-1]: if arr[j]<=pivot then i++, swap(arr[i],arr[j]); swap(arr[i+1],arr[hi])
steps.apply: pivot = arr[4] = 100. i = -1. j=0: 60<=100, i=0, swap(arr[0],arr[0]) — self-swap. j=1: 70<=100, i=1, swap(arr[1],arr[1]). j=2: 80<=100, i=2, swap(arr[2],arr[2]). j=3: 90<=100, i=3, swap(arr[3],arr[3]). Final: swap(arr[4],arr[4]). Pivot placed at index 4. All swaps are self-swaps. Count of distinct-position swaps = 0.
steps.note: Self-swaps (same index) are no-ops and do not count as swaps in the minimum-swap sense.

steps.title: Recurse on left sub-arrays
steps.apply: Each sub-array is also sorted. The same analysis repeats: every element is less than the sub-array's last element (pivot), so i ends at hi-1 and the only swap is the pivot with itself. No element changes its position at any level. Total meaningful swaps = 0.
steps.note: An array of one element ([60]) requires no partitioning. Total swaps across all recursive calls = 0.

solution.result: The minimum number of swaps performed is 0.

questions.formula_note: Lomuto partition on an already-sorted array: all elements in [lo..hi-1] satisfy arr[j] <= pivot, so i = hi-1 and the final pivot swap is a self-swap — zero net data movement at every recursive level.

questions.question:
Solve the recurrence equations:
T(n) = T(n-1) + n
T(1) = 1
questions.to_find: Closed-form solution of the recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + n with T(1) = 1

understand.plain: We have a recurrence where the cost of solving a problem of size n equals the cost of solving a problem of size n-1 plus n units of extra work. By repeatedly substituting (telescoping), we get a sum 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2. This is the formula for triangular numbers.

keywords.explain: An equation that defines a sequence where each term is expressed in terms of previous terms.
keywords.example: T(n) = T(n-1) + n means: cost of size n = cost of size n-1 plus n extra.

keywords.explain: A technique where you expand the recurrence step by step until the base case, creating a series of cancellations.

given.aim: Find a closed form for T(n) defined by T(n) = T(n-1) + n, T(1) = 1.

terms.meaning: Recurrence rule: subtract 1 from the problem size each time, adding n work
terms.connects: Telescoping this gives T(n) = sum_{k=1}^{n} k

terms.meaning: Base case: a problem of size 1 takes 1 unit
Sum of first n natural numbers: 1 + 2 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2
terms.connects: Directly gives the closed form after telescoping

given.plan: Telescope the recurrence by substituting T(n-1), T(n-2), ... until T(1), collecting all the additive terms to form an arithmetic series, then apply the closed-form formula for the sum.

steps.title: Telescope the recurrence
steps.note: After (n-1) substitution steps we reach the base case T(1) = 1.

questions.formula_note: Telescoping method applied to a linear first-order recurrence; closed form is the arithmetic series sum formula n*(n+1)/2.

questions.question:
Solve the recurrence equations:
T(n) = T(n/2) + 1
T(1) = 1
questions.to_find: Closed-form solution of the recurrence T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 with T(1) = 1

understand.plain: This recurrence halves the problem size at each step and does 1 unit of work at each level. The number of times we can halve n before reaching 1 is log_2(n). Adding the 1 unit from the base case gives T(n) = log_2(n) + 1. This is the time complexity of binary search.

keywords.explain: A recurrence where the input is split into a fraction of its size at each recursive call.
keywords.example: T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 represents binary search: halve the search space, do O(1) comparison.

keywords.explain: Halving n repeatedly requires log_2(n) steps before reaching n=1.
keywords.example: For n=8: 8 -> 4 -> 2 -> 1, that is 3 = log_2(8) steps.

understand.visual_alt: Recursion tree: T(n) splits into T(n/2) at each level with +1 work; depth = log_2(n); T(n) = log_2(n) + 1.

given.aim: Find a closed form for T(n) defined by T(n) = T(n/2) + 1, T(1) = 1.

terms.meaning: Each step halves n and costs 1 additional unit of work
terms.connects: After telescoping k times: T(n) = T(n/2^k) + k; set n/2^k = 1 to stop

terms.meaning: Base case: the smallest sub-problem costs 1
terms.example: When n=1 the recursion stops with value 1
terms.connects: Substituting k = log_2(n) gives T(n) = T(1) + log_2(n) = 1 + log_2(n)

terms.meaning: The number of halvings needed to reduce n to 1
terms.connects: This is the total depth of recursion, which equals the work done (since each level does 1 unit)

given.plan: Telescope the recurrence by substituting repeatedly, express T(n) = T(n/2^k) + k, then choose k = log_2(n) to hit the base case T(1) = 1.

steps.title: Telescope the recurrence
steps.note: At each substitution, the argument halves and the additive constant increases by 1.

steps.title: Apply base case condition
steps.apply:
Set k = log_2(n) so that n/2^k = 1. Then:
T(n) = T(1) + log_2(n) = 1 + log_2(n)

questions.formula_note: Telescoping a divide-by-2 recurrence with O(1) work per level gives logarithmic depth; this is the recurrence for binary search with T(n) = log_2(n) + 1.

questions.question:
Find a solution to the following recurrence equation:
T(n) = sqrt(n) * T(sqrt(n)) + n
T(1) = 1
questions.to_find: Closed-form solution of the recurrence T(n) = sqrt(n) * T(sqrt(n)) + n with T(1) = 1

understand.plain: This is an unusual recurrence where the sub-problem size is sqrt(n) and there are sqrt(n) such sub-problems. To solve it, we use the substitution n = 2^m (so sqrt(n) = 2^(m/2)) and divide both sides by n. This transforms the recurrence into a simple halving one: S(m) = S(m/2) + 1, whose solution is log(m). Translating back gives T(n) = n * log_2(log_2(n)).

keywords.explain: Replacing n by 2^m converts square root operations into halving of m, simplifying the recurrence.
keywords.example: If n = 2^8 = 256, then sqrt(n) = 2^4 = 16, and m=8 becomes m/2=4.

keywords.explain: Dividing T by the input size n = 2^m cancels the common factor and reduces the recurrence to S(m) = S(m/2) + 1.
keywords.example: S(m) = S(m/2) + 1 is the binary search recurrence, solved by S(m) = log_2(m).

understand.visual_alt: Substitution: let n=2^m, define S(m)=T(2^m)/2^m; recurrence becomes S(m)=S(m/2)+1 with solution S(m)=log_2(m); reverse substitution gives T(n)=n*log_2(log_2(n)).

given.aim: Find a closed form for T(n) = sqrt(n)*T(sqrt(n)) + n with T(1) = 1.

terms.meaning: sqrt(n) sub-problems of size sqrt(n), plus n units of work at this level
terms.connects: After substitution n=2^m, this becomes T(2^m) = 2^(m/2)*T(2^(m/2)) + 2^m

terms.meaning: Replace n with 2^m so sqrt(n) = 2^(m/2), converting the root into a simpler halving
terms.connects: Allows us to define S(m) = T(2^m)/2^m and derive S(m) = S(m/2) + 1

terms.meaning: Normalized recurrence obtained by dividing both sides of T(2^m) = 2^(m/2)*T(2^(m/2))+2^m by 2^m
terms.example: S(m) = S(m/2) + 1; solution is S(m) = log_2(m)
terms.connects: T(n) = 2^m * S(m) = n * log_2(m) = n * log_2(log_2(n))

given.plan: Substitute n = 2^m to convert the square-root recurrence. Define S(m) = T(2^m)/2^m, derive S(m) = S(m/2) + 1, solve it as log_2(m), then reverse substitute to get T(n) = n*log_2(log_2(n)).

steps.title: Substitute n = 2^m
steps.formula_raw: Let n = 2^m, so sqrt(n) = 2^(m/2), and m = log_2(n)
steps.note: The key step: dividing by 2^m = n cancels the sqrt(n) factor and reduces to a clean halving recurrence.

steps.title: Solve S(m) = S(m/2) + 1
steps.formula_raw: S(m) = log_2(m)  (standard telescoping of halving recurrence, S(1)=0 for large n)
steps.note: The base case T(1)=1 corresponds to m->0; for the asymptotic closed form the constant is absorbed.

steps.title: Reverse substitute back to n
steps.note: Verify scale: for n=2^16=65536, log_2(n)=16, log_2(16)=4, T(n) = 65536*4 = 262144. This is much less than n^2 but more than n*log(n).

questions.formula_note: Variable substitution n = 2^m combined with normalization S(m) = T(2^m)/2^m reduces this square-root recurrence to the standard halving recurrence S(m) = S(m/2) + 1, giving T(n) = n*log_2(log_2(n)).
